perturbation.original_text: The Customizations shall fulfill or exceed all of the functional, performance and other specifications described in the Program Materials and the documents prepared either by Bank of America or Supplier pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Customizations should aim to fulfill the functional, performance and other specifications described in the Program Materials and the documents prepared either by Bank of America or Supplier pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall" to "should aim to" weakens the obligation, making it non-mandatory. This creates ambiguity around the required level of compliance with the specifications.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts contract law principles, specifically regarding express warranties. UCC § 2-313 states that any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. By changing 'shall' to 'should aim to', the customization requirements are no longer guaranteed and introduces ambiguity. This makes it harder for Bank of America to claim breach of contract if the customizations don't meet the specifications, as the obligation is weakened.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall" to "should aim to" weakens the obligation for the Customizations to meet the specifications. UCC § 2-313(1)(a) states that any promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the promise. By weakening the commitment to meet the specifications, the modified text contradicts the creation of an express warranty, as the fulfillment of specifications is no longer guaranteed. The original text created a binding promise; the modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially making it harder for Bank of America to claim breach of contract if the customizations do not conform. Therefore, there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: No later than <<enter Customization Documents Delivery Date>>, Supplier shall deliver to Bank of America the documents described below for this Customization. The detailed specifications so delivered shall be incorporated herein when approved by Bank of America.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier will endeavor to deliver to Bank of America the documents described below for this Customization around <<enter Customization Documents Delivery Date>>. The detailed specifications so delivered may be incorporated herein if Bank of America finds them suitable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall deliver" to "will endeavor to deliver" and adding "around" the delivery date weakens the delivery obligation, making the timing less strict. Also replacing 'shall be incorporated herein when approved' to 'may be incorporated herein if Bank of America finds them suitable', makes it subjective to Bank of America's suitability and weakens that contractual necessity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity around the timely delivery of documents, conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which exists in every contract. The original 'shall deliver' established a clear obligation with a specific deadline, but 'will endeavor to deliver around' weakens this commitment. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith. The shift from 'shall be incorporated when approved' to 'may be incorporated if...suitable' allows for arbitrary decisions, thereby also breaching good faith in contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the delivery obligation and the incorporation of specifications, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original text had a clear 'shall deliver' which creates an obligation, but the changed text now states 'will endeavor to deliver around', which weakens the commitment. This weakening affects the good faith in the contract law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing', 'faithful performance of duties', and 'observance of fair dealing standards'. By weakening the delivery obligation, the modified text arguably fails to ensure 'faithful performance of duties', creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If the proposed price Is Increased by Supplier upon delivery to Bank of America of detailed specifications or at any other time hereafter, Bank of America may terminate this Schedule and shall receive a refund of all amounts previously paid hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: If the proposed price is perceived to be Increased by Supplier upon delivery to Bank of America of specifications or at any other time hereafter, Bank of America may consider terminating this Schedule, and a partial refund, if any, will be determined.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Is Increased" to "is perceived to be Increased", "may terminate" to "may consider terminating", and "shall receive a refund of all amounts" to "partial refund, if any, will be determined" weakens Bank of America's rights. This makes the price increase assessment subjective, the termination discretionary, and the refund uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Consideration and Remedies for Breach
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification makes the remedy for a breach of contract ambiguous and uncertain. Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 344 outlines remedies intended to protect the injured party's expectation interest, reliance interest, and restitution interest. By changing the refund obligation to a discretionary 'partial refund, if any,' the contract fails to provide a clear and predictable remedy for Bank of America, potentially depriving them of their expectation and restitution interests and violating the principles of fair consideration in contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities regarding Bank of America's rights upon a price increase by the supplier, contradicting principles of clear contract remedies. The original text provides a clear right to terminate the schedule and receive a full refund, while the changed text makes termination discretionary ('may consider terminating') and the refund uncertain ('partial refund, if any, will be determined'). This ambiguity undermines the principles of fair consideration and predictable remedies as outlined in the provided law explanation, which references Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 344 and the need to protect expectation and restitution interests. The snippet from Wex emphasizes that 'consideration is the main element of a contract' and that contracts without clear consideration may not be enforceable.
